channel.title: Existentialism on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Existentialism on emsenn.net

item.title: Absurdism
item.description: Absurdism is Albert Camus’s philosophical position, articulated most directly in The Myth of Sisyphus (1942). The absurd is not a property of the world and not a property of human consciousness. It arises from their confrontation: humans desire meaning, coherence, and purpose; the universe is indifferent to that desire. The absurd is the gap between the two — not resolvable by eliminating either side, because doing so would eliminate the confrontation that constitutes it.

item.title: Active Nihilism
item.description: Active nihilism is Friedrich Nietzsche’s term for the form of nihilism that responds to the collapse of metaphysical foundations not with exhaustion or despair (passive nihilism) but with the active destruction of old values and the creation of new ones. It is nihilism as a force, not as a condition — the hammer, not the rubble. Nietzsche’s image in Twilight of the Idols (1889) — “philosophizing with a hammer” — captures the stance: the old idols are hollow, and the hammer’s tap reveals them as such. Active nihilism is the willingness to tap.

item.title: Existentialism
item.description: Existentialism is the philosophical tradition organized around the claim that existence precedes essence: human beings are not born with a fixed nature or purpose but constitute themselves through their choices, actions, and commitments in concrete situations. There is no human essence that determines what a person should do or become. What a person is emerges from how they live, and the responsibility for that emergence cannot be deflected onto God, nature, society, or history.

item.title: Nihilism
item.description:
Nihilism is the philosophical confrontation with the claim that life, the universe, or human values have no inherent meaning, purpose, or foundation. The term derives from the Latin nihil (nothing) and entered philosophical discourse primarily through Friedrich Nietzsche’s analysis of the “death of God” — the collapse of the metaphysical and religious frameworks that had grounded European moral and intellectual life for two millennia.

Nietzsche’s diagnosis in The Gay Science (1882) is specific: it is not that God has been disproved but that the entire structure of transcendent meaning — the conviction that the world has a purpose, that suffering is redeemed, that moral law is grounded in something beyond human convention — has become unbelievable. The “death of God” names the historical event in which European culture loses the capacity to take its own metaphysical foundations seriously. What follows is nihilism: the condition in which the old values have lost their authority and no new values have taken their place.
